worth_it_explanation: Amazon DSP spans a wide range of inventory from Amazon-owned premium placements (Prime Video, streaming TV (STV), IMDb, Fire TV) to open exchange display at very different quality and cost tiers. A single flat bid set at the line-item level competes on all of it at once, which means either overbidding on cheap exchange inventory or losing auctions on the premium placements that drive the strongest outcomes. Differentiating bids by supply source takes 15 to 30 minutes per line item and has a direct effect on where spend lands and what the CPM delivers.
intro: Amazon DSP buys inventory across Amazon-owned properties, Amazon Publisher Direct (direct relationships with third-party publishers), and open third-party exchanges. These supply sources differ substantially in audience quality, viewability, ad experience, and clearing price. Streaming TV and Prime Video placements clear at CPMs well above display exchange inventory, and Amazon-owned inventory carries different competitive dynamics from third-party supply. These checks confirm that bids reflect those differences, that high-priority supply is not bid out of reach, and that premium Amazon-owned placements are not being overpaid relative to what the campaign goal requires.

audit_checks.title: Line items use one flat bid across all supply sources regardless of quality tier
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A flat bid treats a verified Prime Video placement and open exchange display as interchangeable. The flat rate is either too high for exchange inventory, inflating CPMs without a corresponding lift in outcome quality, or too low for premium placements, causing the line item to lose those auctions while filling on lower-quality supply.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP line items bid against multiple supply sources simultaneously. Amazon owned-and-operated inventory (Prime Video, streaming TV, IMDb, Fire TV, Amazon.com display), Amazon Publisher Direct (direct deals with third-party publishers), and open real-time bidding (RTB) exchanges all compete for the same bid. These sources clear at very different price levels. Streaming TV and Prime Video placements typically command CPMs several times higher than open exchange display, reflecting the quality of the inventory, the non-skippable ad experience, and the depth of Amazon's first-party audience data attached to the placement.

When a line item carries a single base bid, that bid competes on all supply sources simultaneously. A bid calibrated to win open exchange display will rarely win streaming TV or Prime Video auctions, where clearing prices are structurally higher. A bid set to win premium placements will overpay on exchange inventory where the same audience may be available at a lower cost.

Amazon DSP allows bid adjustments at the supply source level within a line item, and separate line items can be scoped to specific supply types. Either approach lets bids reflect the actual value and clearing-price reality of each environment. A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The line item's delivery report shows a mix of premium Amazon-owned and open exchange impressions but a single CPM across both
  • The line item is winning few or no impressions on Amazon-owned premium placements despite the supply being available
  • The effective CPM on exchange-sourced impressions is close to the effective CPM on Amazon-owned impressions, even though the two environments clear at different levels
You may want to review line items serving across inventory tiers and consider whether the bid reflects the different competitive dynamics of each supply source.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers focused on Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN) purchases, Amazon-owned placements come with Amazon attribution built in and reach audiences during active shopping sessions. You may want to set a higher bid on Amazon-owned supply where the purchase-intent signal is strongest and the attribution path is clearest.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations, the bid premium for Amazon-owned inventory is justified only if the audience quality and reach there materially outperforms exchange supply for the specific goal. You may want to compare cost-per-outcome across supply sources before committing a higher bid to Amazon-owned placements by default.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Review the line item's supply source configuration and delivery report to determine whether a flat bid is being applied across materially different inventory tiers.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the line item and navigate to its supply configuration. Note whether bid adjustments are set at the supply source level or whether a single base bid applies to all sources.

Pull the line item's delivery report segmented by supply source. Compare the impression count and effective CPM across Amazon-owned placements (Prime Video, streaming TV, Fire TV, Amazon.com) versus third-party exchange inventory.

If Amazon-owned impressions represent a small share of delivery despite being available, note the base bid and compare it to the typical clearing price for that supply type. A bid below the clearing price will lose most auctions for that source.
Note any supply sources where the effective CPM is unexpectedly close across very different inventory tiers, which may indicate the bid is not differentiated.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether each line item uses a flat bid across all supply sources or sets bid adjustments by supply type. A line item with bid adjustments that reflect the relative clearing prices and outcome value of each supply tier passes. A line item with a single flat bid serving a meaningful mix of premium Amazon-owned and open exchange inventory fails, recorded with the line item name and the supply source breakdown from the delivery report.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the supply source bid configuration and the delivery report breakdown for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that line items on {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are bidding a single flat rate across all supply sources, including both premium Amazon-owned placements and open exchange inventory. These environments clear at materially different CPMs, so a flat bid causes the campaigns to win few auctions on higher-quality Amazon-owned supply while overpaying or overbidding on lower-tier exchange inventory. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each line item's supply source mix and setting bid adjustments that reflect the competitive dynamics and outcome value of each supply tier, or scoping separate line items to specific supply types where the goals differ.

audit_checks.title: Streaming TV line items are bidding below the clearing price for the supply
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A streaming TV (STV) line item with a bid below the clearing price for the placements it targets will win few or no auctions. The budget is not spent against the intended supply, which means the campaign's reach and frequency goals for that environment go unmet while the issue reads in the console only as low delivery.

educate.base:
Streaming TV placements on Amazon-owned inventory, including Prime Video and Fire TV channels, are non-skippable full-screen video in a lean-back environment with deep Amazon first-party audience data attached. That combination pushes clearing prices above what display or open exchange video typically clears at. Advertisers entering this environment for the first time, or migrating bids from display campaigns, may carry over bid levels set for a lower-cost supply type.

When the bid sits below the floor or below the competitive clearing price for a placement, the line item simply does not win auctions. The delivery report shows low or zero impressions for that supply type, and pace falls short of budget. The cause does not surface obviously in the console as a bid error; it reads as a pacing or delivery issue that could be attributed to audience size, creative, or targeting rather than to the bid itself.

A few signs point to this check applying:
  • An STV line item is underdelivering relative to its budget and the audience size appears sufficient
  • The delivery report shows impression volume well below projections for the targeted supply source
  • The effective CPM on delivered impressions is at the floor but the win rate is very low, suggesting the bid is competing but losing most auctions
You may want to check the bid against Amazon's estimated clearing range for the supply type, which the console surfaces in the line item setup when supply source and targeting are configured.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, STV on Prime Video and Fire TV reaches audiences in a high-attention environment where Amazon's shopping signals are attached to the impression. The bid needed to win these auctions is typically higher than display, but the audience quality and attribution path to purchase may justify it. You may want to compare the STV bid against the clearing range estimate before concluding the line item is simply underperforming.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, STV CPMs are a significant step up from display. You may want to confirm the campaign budget is sized for the STV clearing range before attributing low delivery to audience or targeting issues.

learn_more.source_title: Streaming TV ads: Advertise on shows, movies, live sports

investigate.intro: Confirm that the STV line item's bid is sufficient to compete in auctions for the targeted supply source by comparing it to the console's clearing range estimate and reviewing the delivery report for win rate signals.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the STV line item and navigate to its bid settings. Note the base bid and any supply source bid adjustments applied.
In the line item's supply configuration, check whether Amazon DSP surfaces a clearing range estimate for the targeted supply type. Compare the current bid to the low end of that range.

Pull the line item's delivery report. Note the impression volume, the effective CPM on delivered impressions, and the pacing against budget. A low win rate alongside an effective CPM at or near the bid floor suggests the bid is competing but losing most auctions.

Compare the STV line item's delivery pace to other line items in the same order that are serving display or non-STV supply at similar audience sizes. Persistent underdelivery on STV while display paces normally points to a bid level issue specific to the supply type.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the STV line item's base bid, the console's clearing range estimate for the targeted supply type, and the delivery report's win rate signals. A bid within or above the estimated clearing range with healthy delivery pace passes. A bid below the clearing range estimate or a line item with low impressions and low effective CPM despite sufficient audience size fails, recorded with the line item name, the bid amount, and the estimated clearing range.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the STV bid, the clearing range estimate from the console, and the delivery report observations for each streaming TV line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more streaming TV line items on {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are bidding below the estimated clearing price for the targeted supply source. The line items are not winning auctions at a rate that would deliver against budget, and the underdelivery reads in reporting as a pacing problem rather than a bid problem. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the bid against Amazon DSP's clearing range estimate for the supply type, adjusting the bid to a level that can compete for the intended inventory, and confirming that the campaign budget is sized for streaming TV CPMs, which are structurally higher than display.

audit_checks.title: Premium Amazon-owned inventory is being bid at a rate the campaign goal does not support
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Overbidding on Amazon-owned premium inventory exhausts budget on high-CPM placements when the campaign goal does not require the premium. The effective cost per outcome rises without a corresponding lift in results, and reach that could be achieved through lower-cost supply is foregone.

educate.base:
Amazon-owned inventory, including Prime Video, streaming TV on Fire TV, IMDb display and video, and Amazon.com display, carries a premium over open exchange supply. The premium reflects the quality of the environment, the first-party audience data attached to the impression, and the reduced fraud exposure on owned-and-operated placements. For campaigns where the goal depends heavily on those factors, the premium is justified. For campaigns with goals that do not differentiate between Amazon-owned and exchange supply, paying the premium reduces efficiency without improving outcomes.

A display remarketing campaign, for example, may reach its audience at similar effectiveness through Amazon Publisher Direct or open exchange placements at a lower CPM. Bidding aggressively to win Amazon.com display placements over exchange display for a remarketing list that is already well-covered elsewhere means the budget buys fewer total impressions for the same spend.

The question is whether the specific outcome goal requires the premium. A few signs point to this check applying:
  • The campaign's cost per outcome is rising and Amazon-owned placements account for a large share of spend
  • The effective CPM on Amazon-owned impressions is substantially above exchange CPMs but the outcome rate per impression is not correspondingly higher
  • The line item is winning a high proportion of Amazon-owned auctions, indicating the bid may be above what is needed to compete
You may want to compare cost-per-outcome by supply source in the delivery report. If Amazon-owned inventory costs more but does not drive a proportionally better outcome rate, the bid for that supply source may warrant adjustment downward.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, Amazon-owned placements often do justify a premium because they reach audiences during active shopping sessions with Amazon purchase intent attached. You may want to confirm the premium is delivering proportionally better ASIN-level outcomes before concluding it is unjustified.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, the rationale for an Amazon-owned premium rests on audience quality and brand context rather than on a direct path to purchase. You may want to compare cost-per-lead across supply sources and let that guide whether the premium bid is warranted.

learn_more.source_title: Amazon DSP: Advertise with a demand-side platform

investigate.intro: Compare cost-per-outcome by supply source in the delivery report and evaluate whether the bid premium on Amazon-owned inventory is producing a proportionally better outcome rate.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Pull the line item's delivery report segmented by supply source. Record the effective CPM and the outcome count (clicks, detail page views, purchases, or the relevant conversion goal) for Amazon-owned placements versus exchange supply.
Calculate cost-per-outcome for each supply source. Note whether the cost-per-outcome on Amazon-owned inventory is close to, or substantially above, the cost-per-outcome on exchange supply.

Check the bid adjustments set on Amazon-owned supply sources. If the bid adjustment is positive and the cost-per-outcome analysis does not support the premium, note the adjustment amount and the supply sources it applies to.
Review the win rate on Amazon-owned supply. A win rate that is very high relative to exchange supply may indicate the bid is above the level needed to compete for those auctions.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the cost-per-outcome by supply source from the delivery report and compares the effective CPM and outcome rate on Amazon-owned versus exchange placements. A bid profile where the premium on Amazon-owned supply is matched by a proportionally better outcome rate, or where the campaign goal specifically requires that supply, passes. A profile where the cost-per-outcome on Amazon-owned inventory is materially higher than on exchange supply without a corresponding quality justification fails, recorded with the supply source breakdown and the bid adjustment levels.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the cost-per-outcome by supply source and the bid adjustment levels for Amazon-owned placements above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP campaigns are bidding at a premium for Amazon-owned inventory that the campaign's cost-per-outcome analysis does not support. The premium CPMs on Amazon-owned placements are not matched by a proportionally better outcome rate, which means the budget is buying fewer total outcomes at a higher cost per result. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the bid adjustments on Amazon-owned supply sources and calibrating them to the cost-per-outcome evidence from the delivery report rather than applying a categorical premium to all Amazon-owned inventory.
